---
title: "Nowcasting Supplemental Poverty Measure thresholds"
subtitle: "A pre-registered 2025 estimate from public microdata"
author:
  - name: Max Ghenis
    affiliations:
      - name: PolicyEngine
    email: max@policyengine.org
date: 2026-07-18
bibliography: ../bibliography/references.bib
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    classoption: 11pt
    number-sections: true
    keep-tex: true
abstract: |
  On July 17, 2026, the Bureau of Labor Statistics reissued six years of
  Supplemental Poverty Measure thresholds after finding errors in the code
  that produced them. The correction moved every published threshold by
  less than 1.6 percent. Auditing our own software against it found
  hand-entered threshold values off by up to 8 percent — and both errors
  had survived for years because no one was diffing published artifacts.
  This paper does three things. It quantifies the correction and
  documents a replication of the BLS threshold methodology from public
  Consumer Expenditure microdata, which reproduces official thresholds
  within 1 to 4.5 percent and surfaces six pitfalls in the public files
  that face any CE researcher. It shows that the price indexation
  microsimulation models use to project thresholds understated actual
  threshold growth by 2.2 percent per year over 2020--2024, because the
  SPM is quasi-relative: BLS re-estimates each year from consumption
  data. And it commits to a falsifiable nowcast of the unpublished 2025
  thresholds — \$41,099.57 for owners with mortgages, \$34,250.70 for
  owners without, and \$40,791.72 for renters — built from a
  backtest-selected blend of consumption growth and composite price
  aging (mean absolute error 1.35 percent per year, against 2.23 percent
  for standard CPI-U aging). BLS publishes the actual 2025 thresholds
  around September 2026; a revision of this paper will report the
  realized errors, whatever they are.
---

# Introduction

On July 17, 2026, the Census Bureau announced it would re-release
Supplemental Poverty Measure estimates for 2019 through 2024, after the
Bureau of Labor Statistics found errors in the thresholds that anchor
them [@census2026statement]. BLS reissued the corrected thresholds the
same day, attributing the errors to the computer code introduced with
its September 2021 methodology change, and re-anchored the series at 82
percent of median expenditures, down from 83 percent, to minimize the
break [@bls2026corrected]. Every published SPM poverty statistic for
those six years — national rates, state rates, demographic breakdowns,
and the research literature built on them — used thresholds that were
wrong by up to 1.6 percent.

We maintain open-source software that carries these thresholds into
policy microsimulation, so we audited it against the correction. The
audit found a larger problem on our side: threshold values transcribed
by hand into our own package matched no BLS or Census publication for
four of the six affected years, with errors up to 8 percent. The two
failure modes are the same failure mode. Reference data that people
transcribe, in agencies or in open-source projects, drifts silently
unless something mechanical checks it against the source.

That observation sets the paper's agenda. @sec-production describes how
BLS produces the thresholds and their revision history, which now
includes two code-correction episodes. @sec-correction quantifies the
2026 correction against the values Census actually published, drawn
from five consecutive P60 reports, and quantifies our own package's
errors alongside it. @sec-replication documents an independent
replication of the BLS methodology from public Consumer Expenditure
microdata: its measured fidelity, the four bugs the exercise found in
our replication code, six pitfalls in the public CE files that face any
researcher who uses them, and a negative result — replication at
survey level cannot detect a sub-2-percent error in the official
pipeline, though mechanical artifact-diffing catches it in a week.
@sec-projection turns to projection: because BLS re-estimates
thresholds each year from a rolling consumption window, the series is
quasi-relative, and the price indexation that microsimulation models
apply understated actual threshold growth by 2.2 percent per year over
2020--2024. A backtest selects a better rule. @sec-nowcast applies that
rule to data that already exist and commits, before publication, to a
numeric estimate of the 2025 thresholds that BLS will publish around
September 2026. @sec-discussion draws the implications for
poverty measurement infrastructure.

# How the thresholds are produced {#sec-production}

The Supplemental Poverty Measure compares a resource unit's post-tax,
post-transfer resources to a threshold that varies by housing tenure
and local housing costs [@itwg2010]. BLS produces the national
thresholds from the Consumer Expenditure Interview Survey; Census
applies geographic adjustments, computes resources, and publishes the
poverty statistics [@garner2021].

Under the methodology the SPM Interagency Technical Working Group
approved in September 2020, the threshold for target year $T$ uses five
years of CE Interview quarters lagged one year — collection quarters
$(T{-}5)$Q2 through $T$Q1 [@bls2026corrected]. Within the estimation
sample of consumer units with children, BLS computes FCSUti expenditures
— food, clothing, shelter (including owners' mortgage-principal
payments), utilities, telephone, and internet, plus imputed in-kind
benefits from broadband subsidies, LIHEAP, the National School Lunch
Program, WIC, and rental assistance — equivalizes them to a
two-adult, two-child reference unit with the three-parameter scale of
@betson1996, updates each quarter to threshold-year dollars with a
composite FCSUti price index, and applies the formula

$$
\text{SPM}_h = s \times \left(1.2 \times \overline{\text{FCSUti}}_E - \overline{\text{SU}}_E + \overline{\text{SU}}_{Eh}\right),
$$

where $E$ is the subsample inside the 47th–53rd percentile band of
equivalized FCSUti, $\text{SU}$ is shelter plus utilities excluding
telephone, $h$ indexes three housing-tenure groups, the 1.2 multiplier
covers other basic goods and services, and the anchor share $s$ is 82
percent as of the 2026 correction, 83 percent before it.

Three properties of this design matter for what follows. First, the
thresholds are re-estimated, not indexed: each year's value reflects
the consumption distribution in its window, so the series moves with
living standards as well as prices. The National Academy of Sciences
panel that proposed this architecture intended exactly that
[@citro1995]. Second, the pipeline is complex enough to fail quietly.
The 2026 reissue is its second code-correction episode: the 2022
thresholds already carried "corrections in the computer code used to
model" in-kind benefits, disclosed in a footnote of the 2022 poverty
report [@p60-280]. Census has separately explored simplifying the
design, estimating that a decennially-anchored variant would have put
2024 SPM poverty at 11.3 percent rather than the published 12.9
[@sehsd2026]. Third, everything BLS consumes is public — the CE
microdata, the CPI component series, the methodology documents — so
the pipeline can be replicated, and its outputs checked, by anyone.

# The 2026 correction, quantified {#sec-correction}

The corrected workbook publishes full-precision thresholds for
2005--2024, with standard errors and tenure population shares, and
states that these are the values Census uses to produce SPM statistics
[@bls2026corrected]. The pre-correction values do not remain on the
BLS site; we transcribed them from the five annual P60 reports that
published them, each year cross-verified against the two consecutive
reports that print it [@p60-275; @p60-277; @p60-280; @p60-283;
@p60-287]. @tbl-correction compares the two series for two-adult,
two-child units.

::: {#tbl-correction}
{{< include tables/correction.md >}}

The 2026 correction: thresholds as published in Census P60 reports
versus the corrected BLS workbook, two-adult, two-child consumer units.
:::

Every change is within ±1.6 percent, with mixed signs: renter
thresholds fall in five of six years, owner thresholds move both ways.
2022 sees the largest downward revision across all tenures. Census will
quantify the effect on poverty rates in a working paper before the
September 2026 annual report; because the changes are small relative to
year-over-year threshold growth, no published year-to-year comparison
of SPM rates appears at risk of reversing sign.

Our own software did worse. The spm-calculator package, which supplies
threshold parameters to the PolicyEngine US microsimulation model,
carried a hand-entered table of "published BLS thresholds."
@tbl-package compares that table to what Census actually published.

::: {#tbl-package}
{{< include tables/package_errors.md >}}

Threshold values shipped in spm-calculator through version 0.3.1,
against the values Census published. The 2019--2020 rows appear to mix
misattributed vintages; 2022--2023 match no publication we could
locate.
:::

Errors reach −7.4 percent, several times the BLS correction. Only 2024
and the 2021 renter value were right. The releases that carried these
values passed continuous integration on every commit: the test suite
asserted the code returned the packaged values, and the packaged values
were the thing that was wrong. Version 0.4.0 replaces the hand-entered
table with data generated from the official workbook, bundled with its
SHA-256 recorded, and adds a weekly job that re-downloads the
workbook and opens an issue on any divergence. Against the live BLS
site, twenty years of packaged thresholds now match within half a cent.

# Replicating the thresholds from public microdata {#sec-replication}

The same package includes a from-scratch implementation of the BLS
methodology over raw CE Interview public-use microdata: it downloads
the quarterly FMLI files, constructs FCSUti, equivalizes, deflates with
a composite index built from CPI component series, and applies the
threshold formula. Before this work, that implementation had never been
validated against official values in earnest — its one comparison test
allowed 5 percent tolerance and was skipped by default. Benchmarking it
against both the pre-correction and corrected series, with the anchor
share matched to each (83 percent versus published values, 82 percent
versus corrected, so anchor choice cannot manufacture a fit), surfaced
four errors in our replication code:

1. **Annualization off by a factor of two.** The FMLI expenditure
   variables report a three-month recall window split across two
   calendar quarters (`*PQ` and `*CQ`); the code treated the pair as
   six months of spending and multiplied by two instead of four.
2. **Telephone double-counted.** The FMLI `UTIL` summary already
   contains `TELEPH` (utilities are defined as natural gas,
   electricity, fuels, telephone, and water); the code added telephone
   again.
3. **Phantom columns.** The mortgage-principal and internet columns
   the code referenced do not exist in FMLI; both silently contributed
   zero. Principal outlays live in `EMRTPNO*` and `MRTPRNO*`; home
   internet has no FMLI summary variable at all.
4. **The wrong formula shape.** The code took per-tenure percentiles
   of the FCSUti distribution. The BLS formula pools the estimation
   band, applies the 1.2 other-necessities multiplier, and swaps a
   tenure-specific shelter-utilities average — a different object.

After the fixes, the faithful variant reproduces official thresholds
within 1 to 4.5 percent mean absolute deviation per year, with no
imputation of in-kind benefits. @tbl-replication reports signed
deviations.

::: {#tbl-replication}
{{< include tables/replication.md >}}

Replication deviations from official thresholds (owners with mortgages
/ owners without / renters), with the anchor share matched to each
reference series.
:::

The deviations drift from positive in 2019--2020 to negative by
2023--2024, consistent with the growing in-kind benefit imputations
that BLS adds to consumer-unit expenditures and this replication does
not. Two conclusions follow. The negative one: the BLS correction moved
thresholds by at most 1.6 percent, inside this replication's noise, and
neither reference series fits systematically better in the affected
years — so survey-level replication could not have caught the BLS bug,
and we do not claim otherwise. What catches that class of error is
mechanical comparison of published artifacts, which now runs weekly.
The positive one: at matched anchors, the 83-percent variant fits the
published series about as well as the 82-percent variant fits the
corrected one, independently confirming that the re-anchoring preserved
series continuity as BLS intended.

## Pitfalls in the public CE files {#sec-pitfalls}

Six properties of the public data, encountered while building the
replication and benchmark, will face any researcher who works with
these files. None is documented in one place elsewhere.

1. **Recall-window semantics.** FMLI `*PQ`/`*CQ` pairs split one
   three-month recall period across calendar quarters; on average the
   previous-quarter share is roughly twice the current-quarter share.
   Annualization multiplies the pair's sum by four.
2. **`UTIL` contains `TELEPH`.** Summing summary variables without
   consulting the variable hierarchy double-counts telephone service.
3. **The 2023 food redesign changes schema mid-window.** Files from
   2024Q2 onward drop the `FOOD` and `FDHOME` summaries for `GROCER`
   (all grocery purchases, food and nonfood); BLS's errata allocates 80
   percent of `GROCER` to food at home, and applies that allocation in
   the official thresholds. Pooled multi-year windows mix the two
   schemas row by row, so the construction must be per record — a
   frame-level column check zeroed food for the newest quarters and
   moved replicated 2025 thresholds four to five percent.
4. **Bundle layout migrations.** CE moved its CSV year-bundles from
   one directory layout to another with the 2022 release, and interior
   packaging varies by vintage; per-quarter archive paths from
   pre-2023 documentation no longer resolve.
5. **The BLS API truncates silently.** Unregistered requests to the
   public data API cap at ten-year spans and return the *first* ten
   years of a longer request rather than erroring. A 2005--2025
   request comes back as 2005--2014, and downstream code sees a valid,
   wrong series.
6. **October 2025 CPI does not exist.** The release was canceled
   during the federal government shutdown; 2025 annual averages must
   be computed over eleven months.

# Projecting thresholds past the published years {#sec-projection}

BLS publishes thresholds with roughly a fourteen-month lag: the 2024
thresholds arrived in September 2025, and 2025 thresholds are expected
in September 2026. Any analysis of current-law poverty — which for
microsimulation models is most analyses — needs thresholds for years
BLS has not published. Standard practice, ours included, ages the
latest published threshold by projected CPI-U.

Because the thresholds are re-estimated from consumption data each
year, price indexation is wrong in principle, and @tbl-backtest shows
it is wrong in practice. For each year 2020 through 2024 we stand at
the prior year's corrected threshold and project one year ahead under
four rules: All-Items CPI-U aging; aging by a composite of the CPI
components matching the FCSUti basket; scaling by the growth of our
replicated thresholds; and a 50/50 blend of the last two. All rules are
scored against the corrected actuals.

::: {#tbl-backtest}
{{< include tables/backtest.md >}}

Mean absolute projection error across the three tenure thresholds, by
target year, projecting each year from the prior year's corrected
values.
:::

Three readings. All-Items CPI-U aging errs by 2.23 percent per year on
average, and the errors are almost uniformly negative: actual
thresholds outgrew headline prices in four of five years, most sharply
in 2023, when the CE window's shelter costs compounded faster than the
all-items index. The composite price index closes most of the gap —
the FCSUti basket is nearly half shelter — but stays biased low in
2022--2024. The replication growth ratio, which is the only rule that
tracks consumption rather than prices, wins 2024 outright (0.62
percent) and loses 2023; blending it equally with the composite index
gives the best average, 1.35 percent, and the best worst-year.

The comparison understates the case for the consumption-based rules in
one respect. The two price rules use realized index values —
information unavailable when a real projection is made, as anyone who
projected 2022 from consensus 2021 inflation forecasts remembers. The
replication ratio uses CE microdata that BLS publishes well before the
corresponding thresholds, so in the nowcasting position the data it
needs genuinely exist. The rules also share a residual: every rule is
biased low in 2022--2024, reflecting real consumption growth beyond
what a five-year averaging window passes through and in-kind benefit
expansions that neither prices nor our unimputed replication capture.
With five backtest years we leave that bias unmodeled rather than fit
a constant to it.

# A pre-registered 2025 nowcast {#sec-nowcast}

The 2025 thresholds are a nowcasting target, not a forecasting one:
their entire CE window (2020Q2 through 2025Q1) and eleven of twelve
months of 2025 CPI are published. We apply the blend rule to the
corrected 2024 base. The replication component computes thresholds for
2024 and 2025 with identical code over their respective windows and
takes the ratio, per tenure — level biases that persist across
adjacent, 80-percent-overlapping windows cancel, and the anchor share
cancels exactly. The price component is the FCSUti-composite ratio of
2025 to 2024 annual averages, with 2025 averaged over its eleven
published months. @tbl-nowcast states the estimate.

::: {#tbl-nowcast}
{{< include tables/nowcast.md >}}

The pre-registered nowcast of 2025 SPM thresholds, two-adult,
two-child consumer units, with per-tenure components. These are model
estimates, not BLS publications.
:::

The consumption signal is doing real work: replicated thresholds grew
4.6 to 6.1 percent by tenure against 3.5 percent composite price
growth, so the blend projects 2025 threshold growth of 4.0 to 4.8
percent, against the 2.5 percent that CPI-U-projection aging assumes.
Applied in the PolicyEngine US microsimulation model, the difference
raises projected 2025 SPM poverty by 0.4 percentage points; we treat
that as a sensitivity, not a headline, since modeled baseline levels
carry their own calibration error.

Evaluation is committed here, in advance. When BLS publishes the
actual 2025 thresholds, expected around September 2026, a revision of
this paper will report the signed percent error per tenure and the
mean absolute error of this nowcast, alongside the same errors for
All-Items CPI-U aging and each component rule, computed from the same
base. The packaged estimate is fixed in the repository with its method,
components, and hash; the backtest's error range for the blend, −0.6
to −2.4 percent per year, is our stated expectation of its accuracy.
If the nowcast misses by more than the rules it beat in backtest, that
result publishes too.

# Discussion {#sec-discussion}

The 2026 correction cost the SPM six years of restated statistics, and
the interesting question is not how BLS erred but why the error took
five years to surface. The threshold pipeline's inputs are public, its
methodology is documented, and its outputs are three numbers a year.
Nothing checked them. Our package's larger errors have the same
anatomy: values a person typed, tests that enshrined the typing, and
no process comparing the artifact to its source. Both failures were
detectable by a diff — one that took a scheduled job and an afternoon
to build, and that has no false-negative mode for this error class
short of the source itself being wrong.

Replication plays a different role than we expected when we started.
At 1-to-4.5-percent fidelity, an independent implementation cannot
adjudicate sub-2-percent errors in the official series, and claiming
otherwise would overreach. What it can do is structural: confirm the
formula, the window, and the re-anchoring behave as documented;
catch transcription-scale errors instantly; and — the use this paper
puts it to — measure the consumption growth that price indexation
misses, where a level bias that cancels in ratios stops being a
disqualification. The nowcast is the constructive payoff of
infrastructure built for verification.

For microsimulation practice, the projection result is the actionable
one. Models that age SPM thresholds by projected CPI-U have
understated threshold growth by about two percentage points per year
in the recent period, which flows directly into understated current-law
poverty projections and, near program cliffs, into misplaced poverty
transitions. The blend rule is implementable from public data with a
published, tested codebase, and it degrades gracefully: past the CE
data horizon it reduces to composite-price aging, which alone removed
a third of CPI-U aging's error in backtest.

The pattern generalizes past poverty measurement. Statistical agencies
publish reference series; models embed them; both sides revise. Every
embedded copy is a place where the numbers can silently diverge from
the source, and the fix — bundle the source artifact, generate the
values from it, diff it on a schedule — costs less than one manual
audit. We found errors on both sides of that interface in the same
week. We do not think the interface is unusual; we think it is rarely
checked.

# Reproducibility

Everything in this paper regenerates from public data. The
spm-calculator repository (PolicyEngine/spm-calculator, version 0.4.0)
contains the corrected and pre-correction threshold series with the
official workbook and its SHA-256; the replication, benchmark,
backtest, and nowcast scripts; and the weekly drift-check workflow.
The paper repository pins the exact artifacts behind every table, and
each table in this document is generated from them by script — the
prose cannot drift from the data without the build failing. The
analysis was produced with Claude (Fable 5, Anthropic); every number
derives from the cited artifacts.

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
